text:
This paper deals with the historical, political and emotional aspects of border death reporting – the premature death of migrants whose migratory status has been rendered illegal. Although border deaths have become an integral part of the European migration management, they are systematically excluded from state-level mortality statistics. Consequently, border deaths have to be reported by actors from civil society such as United Nations specialized agencies and non-governmental organizations (NGO) as well as by migrant and anti deportation activist networks.

Refugee deaths: Beyond the humanitarianisation of borders? explores the work of two recent large scale projects recording deaths of refugees undertaken by UNITED – List of Refugee Deaths (of 1990s anti-racist movement) and International Organisation for Migration (IOM), The Missing Migrants Project (more recent humanisation of borders and migration industry). It focuses on how the approaches, methods, terminology and politics underlying these projects construct mortality of refugees, contrasting UNITED’s politically charged framing of deaths as being caused by ‘death by policy’, with the IOM’s focus on deaths that occur during migrants’ ‘journey’ and while campaigning against smuggling in ways that can downplay or obscure the state involvement.

This article engages with feminist, decolonial and abolitionist perspectives, examining the possibilities and limitations of quantification, and whether victimisation lists reduce victims to commodified representations and reproduce racist power structures. It then shifts the focus from data sets to a “bottom-up” practice of documentation by families and survivors of victims. The article foregrounds the emotional underpinnings of this practice, including grief, anger and solidarity, and understands the act of naming victims as a form of politics of grievability.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
